planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring after soil warms in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Shenandoah switchgrass develops its best fall red color in full sun — in shade or with excessive fertility, fall color is less vivid and the compact habit becomes loose.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Shenandoah is the most popular compact switchgrass cultivar — a German selection of Panicum virgatum that stays a neat 24–36 inches tall with an upright, fine-textured habit and produces one of the most spectacular fall color transformations available in any ornamental grass. Beginning in August and intensifying through October, the fine-bladed green foliage transitions through wine-red to deep scarlet-red, creating a glowing color display against the delicate clouds of airy pink-red seed heads. Switchgrass is a native North American prairie grass naturally adapted to a wide range of conditions from wet bottomlands to dry upland prairies across the Gulf Coast region, and Shenandoah maintains this native adaptability while providing improved compactness and superior fall color compared to unselected species. It is highly drought-tolerant once established, needs no supplemental fertilization in most soils, and is resistant to deer browsing. The airy seed heads are wildlife food for sparrows, towhees, and juncos in winter. Cut back to 4–6 inches in late winter (February in zone 9) before new growth begins. Shenandoah performs best in lean to average soils; rich, heavily amended soils cause floppy stems and reduced fall color intensity.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Northwind's strong, upright habit and resistance to flopping make it suitable for windier positions where lower-stature grasses might lodge.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Northwind is the premier tall, strongly upright switchgrass cultivar — reaching 4–5 feet tall with an exceptionally stiff, soldier-straight, non-flopping habit that makes it outstanding for use as a vertical accent, tall screen, or architectural mass planting. The wide, flat, blue-green blades are distinctive and hold their upright posture better through summer heat and fall rains than almost any other switchgrass cultivar. In fall the foliage transitions to yellow-gold, and the airy, open panicle seed heads remain attractive through winter. Northwind was introduced by Roy Diblik of Northwind Perennial Farm in Wisconsin and has become one of the standard switchgrass selections for landscape use due to its exceptional habit consistency. The strong upright form combines beautifully with contrasting horizontal-habit companions like coneflowers and salvias, and the blue-green summer color is cool and sophisticated. Being a native grass, it requires no supplemental fertilization, minimal irrigation once established, and is highly adaptable to the Gulf Coast's clay soils and occasional wet periods. Birds feed on the seeds in winter. Cut back to 6 inches in February in zone 9.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Morning Light performs best in full sun — adequate moisture is important during summer in zone 9, where extended drought combined with reflected heat can stress plants.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Morning Light is widely considered one of the most elegant ornamental grasses available — a refined, fine-textured miscanthus producing very narrow (one-eighth-inch wide) blades edged with a thin white margin that gives the entire plant a silvery-white luminous quality, almost as if the plant is lit from within. The effect is subtle but beautiful: from a distance, the plant appears silver-white; close up, each blade shows a green center stripe and cream-white margins. The narrow, refined foliage and compact, vase-shaped habit reach 4–6 feet, substantially smaller than the larger maiden grass selections. In late summer and fall, feathery, copper-pink plumes emerge above the foliage and last well into winter. Morning Light is notable among miscanthus for being sterile or low-seed-producing in most conditions, which reduces the invasive spread concern that affects some miscanthus selections in the Southeast. In zone 9, flowering typically occurs in September–October; the plant grows as an evergreen or semi-evergreen, requiring cutting back in late winter. An outstanding specimen or mass-planting grass for full-sun locations where its distinctive silver-white effect can be appreciated.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Zebrinus requires full sun to develop and maintain its distinctive horizontal yellow banding — in shade or partial shade, the bands fade and the plant loses its defining characteristic.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Zebra grass is a dramatically variegated miscanthus selection producing broad, arching leaves marked with horizontal bands of creamy yellow that alternate with the green blade tissue — a unique pattern unlike any other common ornamental grass and instantly recognizable at a distance. The horizontal banding is striking and bold, creating a tropical effect that combines exceptionally well with late-summer tropical perennials, dahlias, and other high-impact plants. The plant grows large — 5–7 feet tall and nearly as wide — creating a bold specimen or backdrop presence in the landscape. In late summer and fall, copper-red plumes emerge above the striped foliage. The horizontal banding (as opposed to the longitudinal white stripes of 'Variegatus') is caused by a genetic trait that develops only after temperatures warm — bands may not be visible in spring and early summer but become vivid through the heat of summer. 'Strictus' (Porcupine Grass) is a very similar selection with a more upright habit and slightly narrower habit than 'Zebrinus'. Zebrinus leans with age — staking or dividing every 3–4 years helps maintain an upright stance. Cut back hard in late winter. Miscanthus can self-seed in some areas of the Southeast; remove seed heads before they disperse if invasive spread is a concern.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Adagio is the most popular dwarf miscanthus selection and is particularly well-suited to smaller residential gardens where the full-size maiden grass cultivars would overwhelm the space.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Adagio is a compact dwarf miscanthus cultivar reaching only 3–4 feet — roughly half the size of standard maiden grass — making it one of the few miscanthus cultivars truly appropriate for small gardens, residential foundation plantings, and mixed borders without dominating surrounding plants. The narrow, fine-textured, silvery-green blades have a thin white midrib, giving the plant a delicate, refined appearance that belies its toughness and heat tolerance. In late summer, Adagio produces proportionally large, showy, silver-pink plumes that appear at about the height of the foliage mass, creating a beautiful late-season display that lasts well into winter. The compact size and early bloom time (earlier than most miscanthus — often blooming in late August in zone 9) make Adagio one of the most ornamentally productive miscanthus selections for the Gulf South, where the late-blooming miscanthus often do not flower before the first frost. Fall color transitions through golden-yellow. Excellent for mass planting, specimen use, or as a soft backdrop for autumn-blooming perennials. Cut back to 6 inches in February in zone 9 before new growth begins.
